answer:
Probably not for this infringement. Rule: 17 U.S.C. §412 — statutory damages and attorney's fees available only if the work was registered before the infringement began, or within 3 months of first publication. Your grace window closed ~mid-April 2026; it's July, so if the pirate distribution started before you register, you're limited to actual damages and infringer's profits — and ongoing infringement counts as "commenced" when it first started, so continued distribution after registration doesn't revive statutory damages against that infringer.

Still register now:

1. Required to file suit at all (Fourth Estate v. Wall-Street.com — registration must be granted, not just applied for; use Copyright Office special handling, ~$800, ~5–10 business days, if litigation is imminent).
2. Statutory damages/fees available against any *new* infringers whose infringement begins post-registration.

Cheaper immediate remedy: DMCA takedown notice to the pirate site's host — no registration needed, often resolves distribution within days.
